summary: Game-cheat package with Microsoft-impersonating Run-key persistence and obfuscated payload
details: On load, launcher.js writes an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' pointing at a bundled OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs wscript stub that silently re-launches `node launcher.js` at every login. The Microsoft-OneDrive filename, registry value name, and 'Runtime Broker' process title are deliberate impersonation of Microsoft components to evade user detection — this is malware-style persistence, not a legitimate CLI auto-start. launcher.js also reads server.obf.js from disk and passes it to eval(); server.obf.js is an obfuscator.io-style bundle with a 397-entry RC4-decoded string array hiding all URLs, registry-access commands, and the license-server domain. The deobfuscated code collects the Windows MachineGuid (HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography\MachineGuid), os.hostname(), and the C: volume serial, SHA-256-hashes them into an HWID, and POSTs {key, hwid, nonce} to a concealed LICENSE_URL. The package additionally bundles sens.node (6.8 MB Windows PE) exporting Aimbot/Triggerbot/Teleport/VehicleSpawn functionality, with a license-gated 'Cheat' UI (public/index.html) that auto-hides when a YouTube window is focused (anti-streamer detection). Installing this package establishes hidden persistence on the host, executes opaque code that fingerprints the machine and sends identifiers to an obfuscated remote endpoint, and ships third-party-EULA-violating cheat software that exposes the user to anticheat bans and legal risk.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
